title: Legendrian Non-Squeezing via Microsheaves
speaker: Eric Kilgore
abstract: In this talk I will explain some quantitative embedding results for Legendrian submanifolds of pre-quantization spaces. To start, I will recall some contact non-squeezing results for domains, and present an elementary proof of Legendrian non-squeezing for lifts of integral Lagrangian loops in T*R, using some variations on normal rulings. Then I will explain how to generalize this to high dimensions using the language of microsheaves. If time permits, as an application, I will show that the Legendrian lifts of certain monotone Lagrangian tori in ℂn are not squeezable.

proof_stages.label: Motivating non-squeezing problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls contact non-squeezing for prequantized balls and cylinders, contrasts it with Lagrangian non-squeezing, and formulates the desired Legendrian lift version in a prequantization.

proof_stages.label: Need for new Legendrian obstructions
proof_stages.summary: A recent three-dimensional Ruzelle–Sullivan type obstruction and the failure of symplectic-homology or displacement-energy methods for arbitrary Legendrians motivate replacing existing techniques by normal rulings and, later, microsheaves.

proof_stages.label: Normal rulings in one-jet space
proof_stages.summary: For fronts in the ordinary one-jet space, a normal ruling is defined as a continuous fiberwise matching of strands with fixed cusps and mod-2 unlinked switching rules at crossings, whose count is a Legendrian isotopy invariant.

proof_stages.label: C- and S-rulings in the prequantization
proof_stages.summary: In the S^1-valued jet model of the prequantization of the plane, the ruling notion splits into C-rulings that only match strands and S-rulings that also remember homotopy classes of connecting paths, with crossing rules read in the universal cover and invariant counts for both.

proof_stages.label: Area-one comparison and three-dimensional criterion
proof_stages.summary: If the Legendrian projects into an area-one box or disk, angular separation of matched strands cannot wind around the circle, so every C-ruling has a unique short-path enhancement to an S-ruling, giving #C ≤ #S and hence a nonsqueezing obstruction when this inequality fails.

proof_stages.label: Microsheaves as high-dimensional rulings
proof_stages.summary: Because high-dimensional front singularities make combinatorial ruling rules unmanageable, the ruling count is replaced by microlocal rank-one sheaves in the two compactifications ∂(C×W) and ∂(T*S^1×W), with local reduction/sheafification explaining why these objects generalize rulings.

proof_stages.label: Microsheaf comparison theorem and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The same small-domain comparison says that, for Legendrians inside an area-one prequantized cylinder, the C-side count of microlocal rank-one objects is at most the S^1-side count, yielding high-dimensional Legendrian non-squeezing criteria and applications, and in Q&A this is rephrased as a comparison of Chekanov–Eliashberg augmentations under a deformation.

examples.name: Compressible ball in standard contact vector space
examples.note: A Euclidean ball in standard contact space is pushed inward by a transverse contact vector field, illustrating why naive local contact non-squeezing fails before passing to prequantizations.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the ball in standard contact vector space to explain the failure of non-squeezing by identifying a transverse contact vector field and compressing the ball along it.
